evidence: Original MCAS used one AOA sensor at a time.
Repeated activation and fixed incremental stabilizer movement created large mistrim risk.
Return-to-service fixes directly target this cluster.
clusters.name: MCAS control-law and stabilizer authority

evidence: MCAS was not treated as an integrated function across certification documents.
FAA oversight failed to surface design changes and assumptions.
clusters.name: Certification, safety assessment, and delegation

evidence: Multiple alerts and indications affected diagnosis and response.
MCAS information was absent or limited in pre-accident crew materials.
Revised procedures and training were part of the return-to-service package.
clusters.name: Human factors, alerts, training, and crew response

evidence: JT610 involved a mis-calibrated replacement AOA sensor that went undetected.
ET302 AOA failure mechanism is disputed between EAIB and NTSB, but erroneous AOA is common.
FAA required AOA sensor system tests before return to service.
clusters.name: AOA sensor, maintenance, and accident-specific initiating events

evidence: House report treats production/cost/schedule pressure as safety-relevant.
Avoiding greater training differences shaped program incentives.
clusters.name: Program economics, schedule, production, and training continuity

evidence: Some certification/design elements remained rooted in the original 737 type certificate.
The larger LEAP engines and 737 continuity goal shaped the need for handling augmentation.
clusters.name: Derivative architecture and changed-product legacy frame

diversity_risk: MCAS/control-law evidence dominates the ranking before competing causal layers are fully tested.
